title: Mongol sack of Baghdad
summary: In February 1258 the Mongol army of Hulagu Khan, grandson of Genghis, breached the walls of Baghdad and put an end to five centuries of Abbasid caliphate. The last caliph, al-Musta'sim, was killed a few days later, according to tradition rolled in a carpet and trampled by horses. The city of Harun al-Rashid, the House of Wisdom, and the Bayt al-Hikma libraries was reduced by fire, sword, and the flooding of its canals. The event marked the definitive end of the classical Islamic caliphate and reshaped the political and intellectual centre of the Muslim world.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Baghdad had been founded in 762 by the Abbasid caliph al-Mansur as a round administrative capital on the west bank of the Tigris. Within a century it was the largest city in western Eurasia, its population perhaps a million, its markets connected by boat and caravan from Cordoba to Guangzhou. The city was a hub of translation and scholarship: the Bayt al-Hikma, or House of Wisdom, had drawn scholars translating Greek, Syriac, Persian, and Indian works into Arabic since the reign of al-Ma'mun in the ninth century.

By the thirteenth century that golden age was long past. Real power had passed to Turkic and Kurdish military commanders and, from 945, to the Buyid amirs, and from 1055 to the Seljuk sultans, who ruled from the shadow of the caliph's palace. Successive caliphs recovered a measure of political initiative in the twelfth century, most notably al-Nasir li-Din Allah, but the wider Muslim world had fractured into rival dynasties: Ayyubids in Egypt and Syria, Zangids and their successors in northern Iraq, Khwarazmshahs in Iran and Central Asia. The Abbasid caliph remained a spiritual figurehead accepted by Sunni Muslims from Morocco to Bengal, but he commanded no great army.

The Mongol threat had loomed for a generation. Genghis Khan had destroyed the Khwarazmian Empire between 1219 and 1221, sacking Bukhara, Samarkand, and Merv. His grandson Mongke, who became Great Khan in 1251, resolved to complete the conquest of the Islamic east and gave the task to his brother Hulagu. Hulagu was ordered to reduce the Nizari Isma'ili strongholds of northern Iran, known to Europeans as the Assassins, and then to bring the Abbasid caliph to submission. His army, perhaps 150,000 strong, was one of the largest Mongol forces ever assembled and included a Chinese siege corps expert in trebuchets and gunpowder incendiaries, Christian Georgian and Armenian auxiliaries, and Muslim Turkic contingents.

Between 1256 and 1257 Hulagu systematically reduced the Nizari castles of Alamut and the Elburz mountains. Their imam Rukn al-Din Khurshah surrendered and was later killed. In September 1257 Hulagu sent envoys to Baghdad demanding that Caliph al-Musta'sim tear down his walls, fill in his moat, and come in person to pay homage. Al-Musta'sim, poorly advised by his vizier Ibn al-Alqami and by the dawadar Aybek, refused, sent back defiant replies, and, according to contemporary reports, boasted that the whole Muslim world would rise if the caliph were touched. He made no serious military preparations.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The Mongol army converged on Baghdad in three columns in January 1258. Hulagu commanded the main force from the east; Baiju Noyan advanced from the north through Mosul; a further contingent moved up from the south. The Abbasid army under the dawadar Aybek marched out to meet Baiju near Anbar on the west bank of the Tigris and was destroyed in mid-January when the Mongols broke the dykes and flooded the ground behind them. Perhaps twelve thousand caliphal troops drowned in one night. The remnants fled back to the city.

By 22 January the Mongol columns had encircled Baghdad. They built a palisade and ditch around the city and mounted trebuchets and mangonels on the northeast wall. Because Iraq lacks the great stones of northern lands, the artillerists were reduced to hurling the trunks of palm trees and, in some accounts, the marble facings ripped from suburban villas. A rain of gunpowder-filled ceramic bombs and naphtha jars set fire to houses inside the walls.

On 30 January a section of the Ajami tower collapsed and Mongol assault parties gained a foothold. The caliph, at last realising the extent of the danger, sent out a series of embassies with gifts, then his own sons, and finally on 10 February came out in person with three thousand notables, judges, and officials, to seek terms. Hulagu received him courteously and had him instruct the remaining population to lay down their arms and gather outside the walls, ostensibly to be counted. Those who obeyed were separated into groups and, over the following days, methodically killed. Those who resisted inside the walls were killed as the Mongol army entered.

The sack lasted for a week from around 13 February. Contemporary chroniclers, both Muslim and Christian, describe scenes of appalling destruction. Ibn al-Fuwati, an eyewitness who later served in Hulagu's administration at Maragha, remembered smoke that darkened the sky. Mosques, madrasas, and hospitals were burned. Libraries were emptied into the Tigris, whose waters were said by later writers to have run first black with ink and then red with blood, a rhetorical image now treated as a symbol rather than literal fact but rooted in real destruction. Estimates of the dead vary wildly, from around 90,000 in some Persian sources to 800,000 or more in later Arab tradition; modern historians incline toward the lower end but agree that a substantial fraction of the city's population perished. Nestorian Christians were spared at the request of Hulagu's Christian wife Doquz Khatun and gathered in a church.

On 20 February 1258 the caliph al-Musta'sim was executed. Mongol custom forbade the shedding of royal blood on the ground, and, according to Persian and Armenian sources, he was rolled in a felt or carpet and trampled to death by horses. His two elder sons were killed with him; the youngest was sent to Karakorum. With him ended the Abbasid line that had ruled from Baghdad for five centuries.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Hulagu did not linger. He assigned the region to a Mongol governor and moved his court to Maragha in Azerbaijan, which became the capital of a new khanate known to modern historians as the Ilkhanate. The astronomer Nasir al-Din al-Tusi, whom Hulagu had brought out of the Nizari castle of Alamut, was made head of a great new observatory at Maragha, staffed with scholars from across the Muslim world and later joined by Chinese astronomers. In this sense some of the scholarly life shattered at Baghdad was reborn under Mongol patronage.

Hulagu's western campaign continued. Aleppo fell in January 1260 and Damascus soon after; the Ayyubid principalities of Syria collapsed. But when the death of the Great Khan Mongke reached Hulagu in the summer of 1260, he withdrew most of his army to the Caucasus to be in reach of the imperial succession. His subordinate Kitbuqa was left in Syria with a small force and was defeated by the Mamluk sultanate of Egypt at Ayn Jalut in Galilee on 3 September 1260 by an army under Sultan Qutuz and his lieutenant Baybars. Ayn Jalut was the first major battlefield defeat suffered by the Mongols and marked the effective western limit of their empire.

Egypt now inherited the mantle Baghdad had lost. The Mamluk sultans installed a shadow Abbasid caliph in Cairo, a distant relative of the last Baghdad caliph, and treated him as a source of Sunni legitimacy for their own regime. That Cairo caliphate lasted, largely ceremonial, until the Ottoman conquest of Egypt in 1517. In the east the Ilkhanate itself converted to Islam under Ghazan Khan in 1295 and became patron of a Persian cultural revival that produced the historian Rashid al-Din, the Jami' al-tawarikh, and the Ilkhanid architecture of Tabriz and Sultaniyya. Iraq, however, never fully recovered. The centuries of investment in canals, dykes, and levees that had made lower Mesopotamia the granary of the Abbasid caliphate were broken by war and neglect; population and agricultural yield in the Tigris-Euphrates plain would not return to twelfth-century levels for many centuries.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The sack of Baghdad became a landmark in the historical imagination of the Islamic world. Sunni theologians and jurists debated for centuries whether the caliphate could survive its extinction, and if so how. Some argued that any powerful Muslim ruler who defended the community and applied the law fulfilled its functions. This ideological accommodation opened the way for later universal-caliphate claims by the Ottomans and, in the twentieth century, for movements that dreamed of the caliphate's restoration.

The intellectual damage is harder to measure. The wholesale destruction of libraries and observatories was undoubtedly devastating, but recent scholarship has resisted the older narrative that 1258 alone ended the classical Islamic scientific tradition. Many manuscripts had been copied and dispersed; the Maragha observatory and later Samarkand under Ulugh Beg carried on advanced astronomy, and Cairo, Damascus, Fez, Bukhara, and Delhi remained major centres of learning. What the sack did do, unambiguously, was to end the primacy of Baghdad and to reorient the geography of the Islamic world toward Cairo, Anatolia, and Central Asia.

In western memory the sack of Baghdad supplied a lasting image of catastrophic destruction. Marco Polo, travelling to the Mongol court a few years later, retold the story of the caliph starved to death in his treasure room, a fable that circulated widely in medieval Europe. In modern times the phrase Sack of Baghdad has been reached for whenever the city has suffered in war, and the image of manuscripts thrown into the Tigris continues to stand as shorthand for the fragility of intellectual heritage. It is a measure of the event that eight centuries on it still shapes how the Muslim world remembers the meeting between its scholarly civilisations and the steppe.

worldContext.text: In western Europe the 1250s were a decade of dynastic churn. King Louis IX of France had returned from his failed crusade to Egypt in 1254 and was rebuilding his authority; in 1258 he signed the Treaty of Paris with Henry III of England. In England 1258 saw the Provisions of Oxford, in which a baronial reform party led by Simon de Montfort forced the king to accept a council. In Germany the Great Interregnum divided the throne between rival claimants.

The Delhi Sultanate under Sultan Nasir al-Din Mahmud, guided by his powerful deputy Ghiyas al-Din Balban, kept a wary distance from the Mongols; a series of raids on the Punjab in the 1240s and 1250s was contained. In the south the Yadava kingdom of Devagiri and the Kakatiya kingdom of Warangal were at their height, and the Chola state had disintegrated into successor kingdoms.

In Egypt the Mamluk sultanate had just replaced the last Ayyubid ruler in 1250. Its army would go on to defeat the Mongols at Ayn Jalut in 1260. In West Africa the Mali Empire under Sundiata Keita's successors was consolidating control of the trans-Saharan gold routes. Along the Swahili coast city-states like Kilwa continued Indian Ocean commerce; in the Ethiopian highlands the new Solomonic dynasty had recently displaced the Zagwe.

Along the Mississippi Cahokia was past its peak but still one of the largest cities north of Mexico. In the Valley of Mexico small city-states occupied the ruins of the Toltec world; the Mexica had not yet migrated south. In the central Andes small polities ruled the highlands. In the American Southwest ancestral Pueblo people were beginning the shift from Chaco to Mesa Verde and the northern Rio Grande, aggravated by prolonged drought.

In Aotearoa Maori settlers were adapting to a temperate climate; on Rapa Nui moai construction continued at a great scale. In Melanesia and highland New Guinea intensive taro and sweet potato agriculture supported dense populations. Polynesian voyagers maintained connections between the far islands of the eastern Pacific, and Aboriginal Australians maintained the long-established patterns of managed burning that shaped their ecosystems.

figures.name: Hulagu Khan
figures.role: Grandson of Genghis Khan and brother of Great Khan Mongke; commander of the Mongol army that took Baghdad and founder of the Ilkhanate
figures.credit: Rashid al-Din, Jami' al-tawarikh, 14th century, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Al-Musta'sim
figures.role: Last Abbasid caliph in Baghdad; executed, according to tradition rolled in a carpet and trampled, on 20 February 1258

figures.name: Ibn al-Alqami
figures.role: Shi'a vizier of the caliph, blamed by later Sunni tradition, with disputed accuracy, for encouraging the surrender

figures.name: Nasir al-Din al-Tusi
figures.role: Persian polymath brought out of Alamut by Hulagu; founded the Maragha observatory under Mongol patronage

figures.name: Doquz Khatun
figures.role: Christian Kereit wife of Hulagu whose intercession is credited with sparing the Nestorian Christians of Baghdad

images.caption: Hulagu Khan in battle, from Rashid al-Din's Jami' al-tawarikh, early 14th century.
images.credit: Anonymous 14th-century Persian illuminator for Rashid al-Din, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The Mongol siege of Baghdad in 1258, from a 14th-century manuscript of Rashid al-Din. Bibliotheque nationale de France.
images.credit: Anonymous 14th-century Persian illuminator, via Wikimedia Commons
